events.title: Oslo Accords Signed
events.detail: Israel and the PLO sign the Declaration of Principles on the White House lawn on September 13, 1993, following secret back-channel negotiations in Oslo mediated by Norway. The accords establish a Palestinian Authority and a five-year transitional process toward final-status talks on borders, Jerusalem, refugees, and settlements. Yasser Arafat and Yitzhak Rabin shake hands before President Clinton. The accords represent the first direct recognition between Israel and the PLO but leave core issues deferred. Oslo II follows in 1995.

events.title: Dayton Peace Accords End Bosnian War
events.detail: The General Framework Agreement for Peace in Bosnia and Herzegovina is initialed at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base in Dayton, Ohio on November 21, 1995, and formally signed in Paris on December 14. Brokered by U.S. envoy Richard Holbrooke, the accords end three and a half years of war that killed approximately 100,000 people and displaced 2.2 million. The agreement creates two entities — the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina and Republika Srpska — under a unified state. NATO's IFOR deploys 60,000 troops to implement the peace.

events.title: Guatemala Peace Accords End 36-Year Civil War
events.detail: The government of Guatemala and the Unidad Revolucionaria Nacional Guatemalteca (URNG) sign comprehensive peace accords on December 29, 1996 in Guatemala City, ending one of Latin America's longest civil wars. UN-mediated over several years, the accords include agreements on indigenous rights, socioeconomic reform, resettlement of displaced persons, and military downsizing. A UN truth commission (CEH) later documents 200,000 deaths, 83% committed by state forces. The accords are considered a landmark but implementation of socioeconomic provisions remains incomplete.

events.title: Good Friday Agreement Signed in Belfast
events.detail: The Belfast (Good Friday) Agreement is signed on April 10, 1998, ending 30 years of 'The Troubles' in Northern Ireland that claimed over 3,500 lives. Brokered by U.S. Senator George Mitchell, UK PM Tony Blair, and Irish Taoiseach Bertie Ahern, the agreement creates a power-sharing executive, cross-border bodies, and a human rights framework. The IRA and loyalist paramilitary groups move toward decommissioning. Endorsed by referendums North and South, the GFA is considered one of the most successful modern peace processes. EU membership underpins the Irish dimension.

events.title: Lomé Peace Accord — Sierra Leone
events.detail: The government of Sierra Leone and the RUF (Revolutionary United Front) sign the Lomé Peace Accord on July 7, 1999 in Togo, ending eight years of brutal civil war. The accord grants amnesty to RUF leaders including Foday Sankoh and establishes a power-sharing government. The peace fails within a year when the RUF kidnaps hundreds of UN peacekeepers. A fresh British military intervention (Operation Palliser) and a reformed UN mission (UNAMSIL) ultimately suppress the RUF. The Special Court for Sierra Leone later tries and convicts Charles Taylor. A cautionary tale on premature amnesty.

events.title: Arusha Peace Agreement — Burundi
events.detail: The Arusha Peace and Reconciliation Agreement for Burundi is signed on August 28, 2000, mediated by Nelson Mandela after Julius Nyerere's death. The accord ends six years of civil war between the Tutsi-dominated army and Hutu rebel groups, establishing power-sharing and transitional institutions. Implementation is incomplete at signing, with major rebel factions excluded. A later ceasefire with the main FNL faction (Palipehutu-FNL) is reached in 2009. Burundi's political stability remains fragile, with a coup attempt in 2015 and ongoing repression under Nkurunziza.

era: War on Terror & Peace Process Challenges (2001–2010)

events.title: Helsinki MOU Ends Aceh Conflict
events.detail: The Memorandum of Understanding between the Government of Indonesia and the Free Aceh Movement (GAM) is signed in Helsinki on August 15, 2005, ending a 29-year separatist conflict that killed approximately 15,000 people. Mediated by former Finnish President Martti Ahtisaari under the Crisis Management Initiative (CMI), the MOU follows the December 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami, which devastated Aceh and created an opening for talks. GAM agrees to demobilize and relinquish its independence demand; Jakarta grants Aceh special autonomy and wealth-sharing for oil revenues. EU and ASEAN monitors verify implementation. Considered a model humanitarian diplomacy breakthrough.

events.title: Nepal Comprehensive Peace Agreement
events.detail: The Government of Nepal and the Communist Party of Nepal (Maoist) sign the Comprehensive Peace Agreement on November 21, 2006, ending a decade-long Maoist insurgency that killed over 17,000 people. The CPA follows the 2006 Jana Andolan II people's movement and establishes a ceasefire, cantonment of Maoist combatants, elections for a Constituent Assembly, and abolition of the monarchy. UN Political Mission (UNMIN) monitors cantonment. The Maoists enter mainstream politics, eventually winning the 2008 Constituent Assembly elections. Nepal transitions to a federal democratic republic. Integration of Maoist fighters into the national army remains contentious for years.

events.title: Darfur Peace Agreement — Partial, Contested
events.detail: The Darfur Peace Agreement is signed in Abuja, Nigeria on May 5, 2006 between the Sudanese government and one SLA faction (Minni Minawi), mediated by the African Union. Two major rebel factions refuse to sign. The government violates the ceasefire within months. The ICC issues arrest warrants for President Omar al-Bashir in 2009 for genocide, war crimes, and crimes against humanity — the first ICC warrant for a sitting head of state. Over 300,000 people are estimated to have died. AU-UN hybrid mission UNAMID deploys in 2007. A new surge of violence in Darfur restarts after Sudan's 2023 civil war.

events.title: Juba LRA Talks Collapse — Uganda
events.detail: The Juba Peace Process between the government of Uganda and the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) collapses in April 2008 when LRA leader Joseph Kony refuses to sign the Final Peace Agreement despite having agreed to its terms. Kony cites fear of ICC prosecution. The mediation by Riek Machar (then Vice President of Southern Sudan) had produced a framework addressing accountability, disarmament, and amnesty. Following the collapse, Uganda, DRC, CAR, and South Sudan launch Operation Lightning Thunder, a joint military offensive. The LRA disperses into Central African Republic and DRC, committing atrocities across a broader geographic area.

events.title: Comprehensive Peace Agreement — Sudan/South Sudan
events.detail: The Comprehensive Peace Agreement ending Sudan's Second Civil War (1983–2005) is signed in Nairobi on January 9, 2005 by the Government of Sudan and the Sudan People's Liberation Movement/Army, mediated by IGAD and the Troika (U.S., UK, Norway). The CPA establishes a six-year Interim Period, wealth and power sharing, and a self-determination referendum for southern Sudan. John Garang dies in a helicopter crash months after becoming First Vice President. South Sudan votes 98.8% for independence in January 2011 and declares independence on July 9, 2011 — the world's newest state. Post-independence conflict erupts again in 2013.

events.title: Colombia–FARC Formal Talks Open in Havana
events.detail: The Colombian government of President Juan Manuel Santos and the FARC-EP revolutionary group open formal peace negotiations in Havana, Cuba on November 19, 2012, after secret exploratory talks brokered by Cuba and Norway. The six-point agenda covers: rural reform, political participation, illicit crops, victims, DDR (disarmament, demobilization, reintegration), and implementation. The talks run for four years, the longest peace negotiation in Colombian history. Havana hosts the talks; Cuba and Norway act as guarantors; Venezuela and Chile as accompanying countries.

sources.name: Colombian Government Office of the High Commissioner for Peace

events.title: Minsk Protocol — Ukraine–Russia (Minsk I)
events.detail: The Minsk Protocol is signed on September 5, 2014 by the Trilateral Contact Group — Ukraine, Russia, and OSCE — along with representatives of separatist 'republics' in the Donbas. The agreement includes a ceasefire, OSCE monitoring, prisoner exchanges, and humanitarian corridors. Fighting continues within days of signing. A follow-on Minsk Memorandum is signed September 19. The ceasefire repeatedly breaks down. Minsk II (February 2015) establishes a more detailed framework, but its implementation is blocked. Russia invades Ukraine fully in February 2022, rendering the Minsk framework defunct. Both sides accuse the other of negotiating in bad faith.

events.title: Algiers Accords — Mali
events.detail: The Accord for Peace and Reconciliation in Mali is signed in Algiers on June 20, 2015 by the government of Mali, the Coordination of Azawad Movements (CMA, including Tuareg groups), and the Platform coalition. Mediated by Algeria with AU, UN (MINUSMA), EU, ECOWAS, and OIC support, the accord provides for devolution, security sector reform, and northern development. Implementation stalls on all fronts. MINUSMA withdraws in December 2023 at junta request. The accord is effectively suspended after the 2021 military coup. The CMA and Platform sign a ceasefire extension in 2023, but hostilities resume as the junta realigns with Wagner/Russia.

events.title: Yemen Conflict Escalates; UN Peace Efforts Begin
events.detail: Following Saudi-led coalition intervention from March 2015, the UN Special Envoy for Yemen Ismail Ould Cheikh Ahmed launches multiple rounds of peace talks — Geneva (June 2015) and Biel (December 2015). UNSCR 2216 (April 2015) imposes an arms embargo on Houthi forces. Talks fail to achieve a ceasefire. Humanitarian corridor negotiations continue at ICRC level. The UN subsequently cycles through multiple special envoys — Griffiths (2018), Grundberg (2021) — as the conflict kills over 377,000 (including indirect deaths) through 2021 per UNDP estimates. The peace process is marked by repeated partial ceasefires and their collapse.

sources.name: UN Office of the Special Envoy for Yemen

events.title: Colombia–FARC Peace Agreement Signed
events.detail: After four years of negotiations, the Colombian government and FARC-EP sign a final peace agreement on November 24, 2016 in Bogotá (after a revised version following the October 2 plebiscite rejection). The agreement covers land reform, political participation, illicit crop substitution, transitional justice (Special Jurisdiction for Peace — JEP), victims, and reintegration. President Santos receives the Nobel Peace Prize. Approximately 13,000 FARC combatants demobilize. However, a dissident faction (FARC-EP Estado Mayor Central, led by 'Iván Mordisco') re-arms. Implementation of rural reform and crop substitution lags. JEP opens investigations into FARC and military crimes.

sources.name: Colombian Government High Commissioner for Peace

events.title: Bangsamoro Organic Law — Philippines
events.detail: The Bangsamoro Organic Law (BOL) is signed by President Rodrigo Duterte on July 26, 2018, creating the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao (BARMM) to replace the failed ARMM. The culmination of decades of conflict and negotiations with the Moro Islamic Liberation Front (MILF), the BOL provides meaningful autonomy, power-sharing, and a decommissioning pathway for 40,000 MILF fighters. The first BARMM parliament is established in 2019. Decommissioning proceeds in phases. Spoiler attacks from the Bangsamoro Islamic Freedom Fighters (BIFF) and Abu Sayyaf Group continue. A landmark in Asia-Pacific peace-building supported by Malaysia as facilitator.

events.title: South Sudan Revitalized Peace Agreement (R-ARCSS)
events.detail: The Revitalized Agreement on the Resolution of the Conflict in South Sudan (R-ARCSS) is signed in Addis Ababa on September 12, 2018, following the breakdown of the 2015 ARCSS agreement and a resumption of civil war in 2016. Mediated by IGAD, AU, and the Troika, the agreement provides for a transitional government of national unity (R-TGoNU), reconstituted army (SSPDF), security arrangements, transitional justice, and humanitarian access. The transitional period is extended multiple times. President Salva Kiir and First Vice President Riek Machar remain in fragile partnership. Implementation of chapter 2 (security) remains the most contested.

events.title: Yemen Stockholm Agreement on Hodeidah
events.detail: UN-mediated talks in Stockholm produce the Stockholm Agreement on December 13, 2018 — the first significant diplomatic breakthrough in the Yemen conflict. The agreement includes a ceasefire in Hodeidah port, a prisoner exchange (the Taiz agreement), and talks on Taiz. The UN Mission to support the Hodeidah Agreement (UNMHA) deploys to monitor the ceasefire. The Hodeidah ceasefire broadly holds but both sides violate its terms. Full port handover to neutral parties is never implemented. The Taiz element collapses. The agreement prevents a full-scale battle for Hodeidah which could have triggered famine conditions for millions.

sources.name: UN Office of the Special Envoy for Yemen
UN Mission to support the Hodeidah Agreement (UNMHA)

events.title: Sudan: Transitional Government After Al-Bashir Ouster
events.detail: Following popular protests that topple Omar al-Bashir in April 2019, Sudan's Sovereignty Council (a military-civilian hybrid) takes power. The AU and Ethiopian mediation produce the Constitutional Declaration of August 2019. IGAD and AU broker the Juba Peace Agreement on October 3, 2020 between the transitional government and several rebel groups (not RSF, which is now part of the state). The process is complicated by the October 2021 military coup by General al-Burhan, which suspends the civilian government. International peace efforts intensify as Sudan descends into civil war between the SAF and RSF in April 2023.

events.title: Doha Agreement — U.S.–Taliban
events.detail: The Agreement for Bringing Peace to Afghanistan is signed between the United States and the Taliban in Doha, Qatar on February 29, 2020, mediated by Qatar. The agreement commits the U.S. to a phased military withdrawal and the Taliban to counter-terrorism guarantees and intra-Afghan talks. The agreement excluded the Kabul government. Intra-Afghan talks open in Doha in September 2020 but stall. The U.S. completes withdrawal by August 31, 2021. Taliban forces capture Kabul on August 15, 2021, 20 years after being removed from power. The Afghan government collapses without a fight. The accord is widely criticized for sidelining the Ghani government and lacking implementation mechanisms.

events.title: UN Secretary-General Calls for Global Ceasefire
events.detail: UN Secretary-General António Guterres issues an unprecedented global ceasefire appeal on March 23, 2020, citing the COVID-19 pandemic as a reason for all warring parties to lay down arms. Over 180 organizations endorse the call. Some 18 armed groups and governments announce temporary ceasefires in contexts including Yemen, Cameroon, Colombia, the Philippines, and Syria. The UN Security Council endorses the appeal via Resolution 2532 on July 1, 2020 (delayed by U.S.–China dispute over whether to mention WHO). Most ceasefires are temporary and humanitarian, not political. The call is nonetheless unprecedented in scope.

events.title: Tigray War Erupts — Ethiopia
events.detail: Ethiopian Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed launches a military operation against the Tigray People's Liberation Front (TPLF) on November 4, 2020, following TPLF attacks on federal military bases. The conflict quickly becomes a humanitarian catastrophe, with Eritrean forces intervening on the Ethiopian side. AU and regional mediation attempts stall for nearly two years. The UN estimates 500,000 deaths from conflict and famine-related causes by 2022. Multiple ceasefire attempts fail before the Pretoria Agreement of November 2022. The war represents the largest armed conflict globally in terms of casualties since the Syrian civil war.

sources.name: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA)

events.title: Pretoria Agreement — Ethiopia–Tigray
events.detail: The Cessation of Hostilities Agreement between the Ethiopian government and the TPLF is signed in Pretoria, South Africa on November 2, 2022, mediated by the African Union (Olusegun Obasanjo, Uhuru Kenyatta, Phumzile Mlambo-Ngcuka). The agreement establishes a permanent ceasefire, disarmament of TPLF forces, restoration of federal authority in Tigray, and unimpeded humanitarian access. A follow-on Nairobi declaration (November 12) sets implementation timelines. Disarmament proceeds incrementally; humanitarian access improves significantly. Eritrean forces' withdrawal becomes a point of contention. The agreement is considered one of the most significant African peace breakthroughs in years.

events.title: Russia–Ukraine Diplomacy Collapses at Istanbul
events.detail: Following Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine on February 24, 2022, brief peace talks are held in Minsk (February 28) and via video (March 2–7). In-person talks in Istanbul on March 29, 2022 yield a preliminary framework: Ukraine proposes neutrality in exchange for security guarantees; Russia offers to pull back from Kyiv. Both sides claim the other withdrew from the deal. Ukraine accuses Russia of using talks as a cover for military repositioning; Russia blames Western pressure on Kyiv to abandon negotiations. Former Israeli PM Naftali Bennett, who mediated, later suggests a deal was possible. No agreement is reached. The conflict becomes Europe's largest war since 1945.
events.confidenceReason: Both parties give contradictory accounts of why Istanbul talks broke down; Bennett's claims are disputed by Ukrainian officials

events.title: Sudan Civil War; Jeddah Talks Launched
events.detail: Fighting erupts between Sudan's Armed Forces (SAF) under General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan and the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) under General Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo 'Hemeti' on April 15, 2023 in Khartoum. The conflict rapidly spreads to Darfur, where RSF and allied Arab militias commit mass atrocities. The U.S. and Saudi Arabia launch the Jeddah Platform mediation talks, producing multiple 'ceasefire declarations' (May, June 2023) that fail within hours or days. By end 2023, over 7 million people are displaced — the world's largest displacement crisis. The African Union launches a separate process (AUHIP). Talks remain stalled as of early 2024 while famine conditions emerge.

events.title: Gaza Ceasefire Negotiations — Qatar/Egypt/U.S. Mediation
events.detail: Following the Hamas attack on Israel of October 7, 2023 (killing ~1,200 Israelis) and Israel's military campaign in Gaza, Qatar, Egypt, and the United States mediate ceasefire and hostage release negotiations between Israel and Hamas. Multiple rounds of talks occur in Doha and Cairo throughout 2024. A temporary ceasefire framework (Phase I) is agreed January 15, 2025 providing for 42 days of fighting pause and hostage releases. The framework repeatedly faces implementation disputes. A Phase II permanent ceasefire remains unresolved as of April 2026. Over 50,000 Palestinian deaths are reported by Gaza health authorities (contested). Saudi Arabia conditions normalization with Israel on credible Palestinian state pathway.

events.title: Colombia–ELN Peace Talks — Continued
events.detail: The government of President Gustavo Petro and the National Liberation Army (ELN) conduct negotiations under a 'Total Peace' framework, with Cuba and Norway as guarantors and Venezuela, Brazil, Chile, and Mexico as accompanying countries. Multiple ceasefire agreements are signed and violated. A 180-day ceasefire beginning January 2024 collapses as ELN and FARC dissident violence continues. Talks resume in Havana. The ELN demands recognition as a political actor and land reform before committing to permanent peace. As of 2026, talks remain active but fragile, with an ELN breakaway faction (Comuneros del Sur) refusing to participate.

sources.name: Colombian Government / Alto Comisionado para la Paz

events.title: Ukraine — Peace Summit & Formula Diplomacy
events.detail: Switzerland hosts a Summit on Peace in Ukraine at the Bürgenstock resort (June 15–16, 2024) with over 90 countries attending, but Russia is not invited and China abstains. The summit communiqué covers nuclear safety, food security, and prisoner exchange but not territorial issues. Ukraine's 10-point peace formula (Zelensky Formula) calls for full Russian withdrawal; Russia dismisses it. Trump's victory in November 2024 U.S. elections injects uncertainty into Western support. Trump envoy Steve Witkoff meets Putin in early 2025. Sporadic direct contact between Russian and U.S. sides continues without Ukrainian participation. A comprehensive peace framework remains absent as of April 2026.

sources.name: Swiss Federal Department of Foreign Affairs

events.title: Myanmar — Ethnic Resistance Advances; Peace Talks Absent
events.detail: The Three Brotherhood Alliance (Operation 1027, October 2023) and broader People's Defence Force (PDF) resistance to Myanmar's military junta (SAC) achieve significant territorial gains in 2024, capturing key towns in Shan State, Rakhine, and Sagaing. China mediates ceasefires between the SAC and ethnic armed organizations (notably the Myanmar National Democratic Alliance Army) but peace talks between the junta and the National Unity Government (NUG) or the broader resistance remain nonexistent. ASEAN's five-point consensus peace roadmap remains unimplemented. China's interests in border stability drive episodic ceasefire pressure.

events.title: Sudan — Peace Process Stalled; Famine Declared
events.detail: By mid-2025, the Sudan conflict has produced the world's largest humanitarian crisis: over 10 million displaced, 25 million food insecure, and IPC Famine declared in multiple areas of Darfur. The SAF and RSF reject participation in a unified mediation process. The African Union-led process (AUHIP), U.S.-Saudi Jeddah Platform, and Qatar-hosted IGAD process remain fragmented and unable to bring both parties to the table simultaneously. SAF retakes Khartoum in March 2025. RSF controls most of Darfur. A humanitarian pause declared in May 2025 breaks down within two weeks. International pressure for accountability intensifies as ICC Prosecutor examines RSF atrocities.

events.title: DRC–Rwanda: Luanda Process & Nairobi Track
events.detail: The eastern DRC conflict intensifies in late 2024 as M23 rebels — widely documented as backed by Rwanda — capture Goma on January 27, 2025. This is a major escalation of a conflict displacing 7 million people. The Luanda Process (AU-mediated by Angola's President Lourenço) and the Nairobi Track (EAC-mediated) operate in parallel. A joint AU/EAC summit produces a ceasefire framework in February 2025 that is repeatedly violated. M23 advances further. Negotiations in Doha (April 2025) bring DRC and Rwanda to direct talks for the first time. As of April 2026, a fragile ceasefire and diplomatic engagement continue but M23 maintains territorial control.

sources.name: UN Group of Experts on DRC
